exhibition_details.proposed_exhibition_title: Tidal Memory: Stories Carried by the Harbor
exhibition_details.exhibition_rationale:
“Tidal Memory: Stories Carried by the Harbor” is a collaborative, mixed-media exhibition that explores how a working waterfront holds, distorts, and slowly releases the memories of the people who pass through it. Our collective—three visual artists and one sound/light artist, all based in Seattle—has spent the last eighteen months interviewing longshore workers, fishers, recent immigrants, and small business owners operating along Elliott Bay and the Duwamish River. The show translates those conversations, field recordings, and archival images into sculptural light installations, textile-based cartographies, and interactive sound pieces that invite visitors to think about the harbor not just as infrastructure, but as a living, remembering organism.

Conceptually, the exhibition is structured around three recurring movements of the tide: arrival, mooring, and departure. Each room or section of the gallery is organized according to one of these phases. In the “Arrival” section, visitors encounter a wall of translucent glass panels (created by Jonah Kim) embedded with found objects gathered from shoreline cleanups—rusted fish hooks, fragments of shipping labels, broken ceramic tiles, and scraps of plastic rope. These are backlit with slowly pulsing LED lights programmed by Marcus Rivera to mimic the rhythm of incoming waves. As visitors move past these panels, motion sensors trigger layered soundscapes composed from harbor noises we recorded at 4:30 a.m.—the metallic clank of cranes, gull calls, snippets of conversation in English, Spanish, Tagalog, and Vietnamese—mixed with whispered phrases from our interviews, such as “I came here in ’89 with one suitcase” and “My father’s hands smelled like diesel and salt.”

The “Mooring” section focuses on what it means to stay rooted in a place that is constantly changing. My own contribution (Elena Martinez) consists of a series of large-scale oil and mixed-media paintings, each built over photocopies of historical shipping manifests and union newsletters from the 1960s through the 1990s. I have partially obscured and partially revealed these documents with layers of paint, charcoal, and beeswax, so that dates, names, and cargo descriptions surface and disappear depending on the viewing angle and lighting. Many of the names on these manifests match the families we spoke with, and I include short handwritten notes directly on the paintings about the individuals who shared their stories with us. For example, one piece, “Manifest 14B: Carmen,” is anchored by the story of a woman whose father worked the docks for 37 years and never once saw the city from the water as a tourist—only as a worker. Viewers will be able to read brief excerpts of her story printed on transparent vellum overlays mounted beside the painting.

In “Departure,” the focus shifts to movement, loss, and the environmental consequences of global shipping. Textile artist Priya Desai has designed a series of hand-dyed cotton and silk banners that function as abstracted, hanging maps of shipping routes from Seattle to ports in Asia and South America. These banners are dyed using rusted hardware collected from decommissioned fishing boats and natural pigments derived from local plants (blackberries, alder, and Oregon grape). The resulting stains and patterns resemble both nautical charts and water damage, suggesting the way trade routes leave indelible marks on both landscape and community. The banners are suspended at varying heights, forcing visitors to navigate around and under them, physically enacting the sense of weaving through crowded shipping lanes or tight-knit neighborhoods.

A key element of the exhibition is interactivity and accessibility. Marcus has created an immersive sound-and-light installation titled “Echo Berth” that occupies a small, darkened room near the end of the gallery. Inside, visitors will find three simple wooden benches facing a semi-transparent scrim. As people sit down, pressure sensors under the benches trigger projections of slowly shifting silhouettes—cranes, container outlines, fishing nets—onto the scrim, while directional speakers play short, layered fragments from our interview archive. These fragments are available in English, Spanish, and Vietnamese, with printed transcriptions and QR codes linking to full-length oral histories hosted on our website. The multi-language approach is important to us, as many of the waterfront workers we spoke with expressed disappointment that their families rarely see themselves represented in local cultural spaces.

Thematically, the exhibition is about labor, migration, and environmental change, but it is also about the small, intimate details that tie people to place: the smell of tar on a father’s jacket, the way foghorns become a kind of lullaby, the ritual of sharing caldo de mariscos after a long shift. We deliberately juxtapose large-scale, infrastructural imagery (cranes, ships, stacks of containers) with small, domestic details (embroidered handkerchiefs, repurposed lunch pails used as lamp housings, Polaroids of family gatherings at the pier). This contrast is meant to challenge the tendency to view ports only through the lens of commerce and logistics, instead foregrounding the human experiences that make these spaces meaningful.

Another important layer of the rationale is our collaboration with a local environmental justice nonprofit, Duwamish River Community Coalition (DRCC). While this exhibition is not formally a fundraiser for DRCC, they have advised us on how to represent the ecological history of the area responsibly and have allowed us to incorporate publicly available data visualizations about water quality, air pollution, and fish consumption advisories. These visual elements appear subtly within the work—for instance, Jonah has sandblasted simplified pollution graphs onto some of his glass panels, and I have embedded small, resin-sealed fragments of EPA reports into the surface of a few paintings. The goal is not to overwhelm viewers with data, but to gently suggest that every personal story we share is intertwined with broader environmental and policy decisions.

We are proposing this exhibition specifically for your gallery because of its flexible lighting, high ceilings, and proximity to the waterfront. We envision the front window area holding one of Priya’s largest textile maps, visible from the street and gently moving when the door opens, acting as an immediate visual invitation. The main central space would host my mixed-media paintings and Jonah’s glass panels, arranged so that the play of natural light during the day and programmed LEDs in the evening creates shifting reflections on the floors and walls, echoing the movement of water. The smaller, more enclosed side room would be ideal for Marcus’s “Echo Berth” installation, allowing visitors a more intimate listening space away from street noise.

From an educational standpoint, we plan to offer at least two public programs during the run of the exhibition: (1) a storytelling evening where two or three of our interview participants share their experiences in conversation with a local historian, and (2) a hands-on workshop in which participants can create small “memory maps” of their own journeys to and from the city, using simple collage and drawing techniques. We hope these programs will attract not only regular gallery-goers but also waterfront workers, union members, and families from nearby neighborhoods who may not typically see themselves reflected in gallery spaces.

In sum, “Tidal Memory: Stories Carried by the Harbor” is an attempt to braid together oral history, visual art, and sound design into an immersive experience that honors the people behind the cranes and containers. By inviting visitors to listen closely, move slowly, and literally navigate around and between stories, we want them to leave with a more nuanced sense of how a harbor remembers—and how those memories, like the tides, are always in motion.
exhibition_details.preferred_gallery_space: Main downstairs gallery with street-facing windows and adjacent small side room suitable for immersive sound installation
